note: Real items pulled from ClinicalTrials.gov, bioRxiv, and ChEMBL. Hooks and explain-beats hand-written in-voice. From here the daily updater appends fresh items automatically.

cards.title: Dosimetry-Based PRRT Versus Standard-Dose PRRT With Lu-177-DOTATOC in Neuroendocrine Patients
cards.hook: Right now most people getting this Lu-177 therapy get roughly the same dose, tumor be damned. This trial actually measures how much radiation each patient's tumor takes up and adjusts the next cycle to match. It's the obvious move, which makes you wonder why it's taken this long to test it properly.
cards.shownBecause: radiopharma — your field, weighted up

explain.snippet: Randomized study comparing dosimetry-guided PRRT against standard fixed-dose Lu-177-DOTATOC in neuroendocrine tumor patients.
explain.gist: PRRT with Lu-177-DOTATOC is usually given as four fixed cycles. This trial images each patient to see the actual absorbed dose and tailors treatment instead of assuming one size fits all.
explain.interesting: If tailoring works, it means some patients are being under-treated today and others are getting exposure they didn't need. Same drug, better aim.
explain.catch: Single-center Phase 2 of 100 people, with the readout running into late 2025. Dosimetry is also fiddly to standardize, which is part of why fixed dosing stuck around.

cards.title: Cells Engage Endogenous Malonate Synthesis to Drive Mitochondrial Metabolism
cards.hook: Every biochem class teaches malonate as the thing you add to a tube to block complex II. This paper says cells make their own malonate on purpose, as an actual metabolic signal. If it holds up a line in the textbook needs rewriting, though 'if it holds up' is carrying weight here since it's a preprint.
cards.shownBecause: biochem — you save a lot of metabolism papers

explain.snippet: Malonate is often described as an endogenous inhibitor of complex II, but the cellular source has been unclear. The authors identify an endogenous synthesis route feeding mitochondrial metabolism.
explain.gist: Malonate has a textbook role as an experimental inhibitor of complex II. The authors show cells synthesize it themselves and use it to tune mitochondrial metabolism.
explain.interesting: That flips a reagent into a regulator. Endogenous metabolite control of the electron transport chain is the kind of finding that spawns follow-up work for years.
explain.catch: Preprint, not peer reviewed, and 'cells make a known inhibitor on purpose' is exactly the sort of clean story that sometimes doesn't survive review.

cards.title: Artemisinin (C15H22O5)
cards.hook: Artemisinin is the antimalarial built around a peroxide bridge, the strained O-O bond most chemists treat as a stability headache. Here it's the whole mechanism. Worth remembering it came out of a plant traditional Chinese medicine had used for centuries before anyone pulled out the active piece.
cards.shownBecause: org chem — matches structures you've opened

explain.snippet: Artemisinin (C15H22O5), a sesquiterpene lactone with an endoperoxide bridge. First-in-class antimalarial, natural product.
explain.gist: A sesquiterpene lactone from sweet wormwood with an endoperoxide (O-O) bridge that most stable drugs would never carry.
explain.interesting: That peroxide is the business end. Iron in the parasite cleaves it and the resulting radicals do the killing, which is why the whole drug class hangs off one unusual bond.
explain.catch: Resistance is creeping up in parts of Southeast Asia, so the 'solved problem' framing doesn't fully hold anymore.

cards.title: Boston Biotech Adds $126M On Top of an Already Large Raise
cards.hook: Boston biotech pulled another $126m on top of the $300m they already had. BMS and Bain put more in before any real data is public, which barely happens right now. Either they know something the rest of us don't, or they've got cash to burn.
cards.shownBecause: startups — funding rounds in your feed

explain.snippet: Series financing; total raised now north of $420m, with participation from Bristol Myers Squibb and Bain Capital.
explain.gist: A Boston biotech added $126m to an already large raise, with a pharma (BMS) and a big fund (Bain) doubling down.
explain.interesting: Insiders adding money before data is public is a real signal in a market where most investors are sitting on their hands.
explain.catch: Big pre-data raises also correlate with expensive programs that need the cash because the science is slow. The signal cuts both ways.

cards.title: Functional Partitioning of Lipoic Acid Decouples Cellular Abundance From Mitochondrial Utilization
cards.hook: Lipoic acid goes into basically every 'mitochondrial support' supplement stack. This paper finds that how much is sitting in the cell has little to do with how much the mitochondria actually put to use. Doesn't prove the pills do nothing, but it does dent the 'more in equals more working' pitch.
cards.shownBecause: biochem — mitochondria topics you engage with

explain.snippet: Lipoic acid is widely included in mitochondrial cocktails, yet its mechanism is unclear. The authors define intracellular partitioning that decouples abundance from mitochondrial utilization.
explain.gist: Lipoic acid gets recommended for mitochondrial disorders. The authors show cellular abundance and actual mitochondrial use are two different things.
explain.interesting: It reframes a popular supplement as a distribution problem, not a supply problem. Taking more doesn't obviously mean more reaches where it's meant to work.
explain.catch: Cell-level work, not a clinical trial, so it says nothing directly about whether anyone feels better on it. Preprint, too.

cards.title: STAMPEDE2: Adding 177Lu-PSMA-617 Earlier in Metastatic Prostate Cancer
cards.hook: STAMPEDE is the giant prostate-cancer platform trial that keeps bolting on new arms, and this one throws Lu-PSMA-617 at men earlier instead of saving it for last. 3,360 patients means whatever comes out the other end will actually settle something. Downside is you wait years for that kind of answer.
cards.shownBecause: radiopharma — your field, weighted up

explain.snippet: Phase 3 multi-arm multi-stage RCT adding 177Lu-PSMA-617, SABR, and other agents to androgen deprivation in metastatic prostate cancer.
explain.gist: A huge platform trial testing whether moving Lu-PSMA-617 earlier in metastatic prostate cancer beats current standard of care.
explain.interesting: Radioligand therapy has mostly been a late-line option. Pushing it earlier, inside a trial big enough to trust, is how it either becomes standard or doesn't.
explain.catch: Primary completion is 2031. Platform trials are powerful and glacially slow, and early-line use means more people exposed if the benefit turns out small.

cards.title: Sub-50 nm Nanopipette Emitters for Native Electrospray Ionization Mass Spectrometry
cards.hook: Native mass spec is great until salt wrecks the spectrum, which is always. This group pulled the spray emitter down under 50 nanometers and got clean protein signal without the usual desalting song and dance. Deeply niche, but anyone who's fought salt adducts at 2am will get why it matters.
cards.shownBecause: wildcard — outside your usual lanes, on purpose

explain.snippet: Cross-platform assessment of sub-50 nm nanopipette emitters for native electrospray ionization MS; reduced salt adduction and source activation energy.
explain.gist: Sub-50 nm glass emitters for native ESI-MS that cut salt adduction, tested across more than one instrument platform.
explain.interesting: Salt handling is the perpetual tax on native MS. A hardware fix that works across platforms is more useful than a one-rig trick.
explain.catch: Emitters that small are fragile and finicky to make reproducibly, so 'works in the paper' and 'works in your hands on a Tuesday' are different claims.

cards.title: Missing Taxane C13α-O-Deacetylases Re-Delineate the Biosynthetic Pathway of Paclitaxel
cards.hook: The paclitaxel biosynthesis pathway has had missing enzymes for years, an awkward gap for a drug this famous. This group says they found the two that were missing and redrew the route. The prize is brewing Taxol in yeast instead of stripping it out of yew bark, assuming the pathway actually reconstitutes end to end.
cards.shownBecause: org chem — biosynthesis papers you've saved

explain.snippet: Discovery of missing taxane C13α-O-deacetylases that re-delineates the biosynthetic pathway of paclitaxel.
explain.gist: Two long-missing enzymes (C13α-O-deacetylases) in the Taxol pathway, filling a gap that has blocked full biosynthetic reconstruction.
explain.interesting: Complete the pathway and you can move paclitaxel production into engineered microbes, away from slow extraction from yew trees.
explain.catch: Finding the enzymes and getting the whole pathway to run in a host are very different milestones, and this is the first one, on a preprint.

cards.title: 177Lu-BetaBart: An Anti-B7-H3 Radio-Antibody Aimed at Ten Tumor Types at Once
cards.hook: Radiopharm Theranostics is putting a Lu-177-tagged antibody against B7-H3 into ten different tumor types in one shot. B7-H3 sits on a lot of cancers, so the wide net has some logic. Still, aiming a first-in-human at ten indications at once reads as either real confidence or not knowing which one will land.
cards.shownBecause: biotech news — radiopharma pipeline moves

explain.snippet: Phase 1/2a of 177Lu-BetaBart, a Lu-177-labeled anti-B7-H3 monoclonal antibody, across ten solid tumor types.
explain.gist: A company is taking a Lu-177 antibody-radioconjugate against B7-H3 into an unusually broad first-in-human basket.
explain.interesting: Antibody-targeted radioligands are the step past small-molecule PSMA agents, and B7-H3 being broadly expressed is the whole thesis.
explain.catch: Ten indications in a Phase 1/2a of about 60 people means very few patients per tumor type. Early signals will be noisy.

cards.title: Unconventional Biocatalytic Strategies Build the Nucleoside Analog Sinefungin
cards.hook: Sinefungin is a natural knockoff of SAM that biochemists have leaned on as a tool for decades, without anyone knowing how the microbe actually builds it. This paper works out the enzymes, and some of the steps aren't in the standard biosynthetic playbook. More of a tool-compound origin story than a drug, but the chemistry is genuinely odd in a good way.
cards.shownBecause: biochem — enzymology, adjacent to your saves

explain.snippet: Unconventional biocatalytic strategies orchestrate synthesis of the nucleoside antimetabolite sinefungin, a SAM analog.
explain.gist: The biosynthetic route to sinefungin, a SAM-mimicking nucleoside antimetabolite, worked out for the first time.
explain.interesting: The enzymes use chemistry outside the usual nucleoside playbook, which is the kind of thing that hands synthetic biologists new tools.
explain.catch: Sinefungin itself is too toxic to be a drug, so this is mechanism and toolkit, not a pipeline story. Preprint.

cards.title: 177Lu-DOTATATE for Recurrent Meningioma: a Randomized Phase 2
cards.hook: DOTATATE is the somatostatin-receptor drug everyone knows from neuroendocrine tumors, and meningiomas happen to carry the same receptor, so the EORTC finally randomized it against standard care in the brain. If it works it hands neurosurgeons a systemic option for tumors they currently just re-operate or re-irradiate. Meningiomas grow slowly though, so the readout is years out and recurring-less is a slow thing to prove.
cards.shownBecause: radiopharma — your field, weighted up

explain.snippet: Randomized Phase 2 (EORTC) of 177Lu-DOTATATE versus local standard of care in recurrent meningioma; 136 patients across 19 sites.
explain.gist: A somatostatin-receptor radioligand borrowed from neuroendocrine cancer, tested head to head against usual care for meningiomas that came back.
explain.interesting: Meningiomas express SSTR2, so a drug built for a different tumor might already fit. A randomized trial is what turns that hunch into standard of care or drops it.
explain.catch: Slow-growing tumor means a long trial and a soft endpoint. Primary completion is 2027 and benefit could be real but modest.

cards.title: 177Lu-DOTA-EB-RGD2: a Radioligand Aimed at Integrins in Recurrent Glioma
cards.hook: This one goes after integrin on the tumor vasculature instead of a tumor-cell marker, and it bolts on an Evans-blue tag that grabs albumin so the drug hangs around instead of washing straight out. Glioma is about the hardest place to land a radioligand given the blood-brain barrier. It is 24 patients and first of its kind, so read it as a signal-hunt and not an answer.
cards.shownBecause: radiopharma — your field, weighted up

explain.snippet: Investigator-initiated Early Phase 1 of [177Lu]Lu-DOTA-EB-RGD2 in recurrent high-grade glioma; safety, dosimetry, and early efficacy in 24 patients.
explain.gist: An albumin-binding, integrin-targeted Lu-177 agent aimed at glioblastoma vasculature rather than the tumor cells directly.
explain.interesting: The Evans-blue albumin trick is a cheap way to extend residence time, and integrin targeting sidesteps the usual delivery problem in brain tumors.
explain.catch: Tiny early-phase study in one of oncology's graveyards. Plenty of clever glioma ideas have died between dosimetry and durable response.

cards.title: 177Lu-Girentuximab Plus Cabozantinib and Nivolumab in Clear Cell Kidney Cancer
cards.hook: Girentuximab targets carbonic anhydrase IX, which sits on nearly every clear-cell kidney tumor, and here it carries Lu-177 on top of a TKI and a checkpoint inhibitor. Stacking a radioligand onto an already-active combo is the aggressive version of the theranostics playbook. The downside is that with three agents at once, a good result will not tell you which one earned it.
cards.shownBecause: radiopharma — antibody radioconjugates in your feed

explain.snippet: Phase 1b/2 of 177Lu-girentuximab combined with cabozantinib and nivolumab in treatment-naive advanced clear cell RCC; 100 patients.
explain.gist: An antibody-radioconjugate against CAIX layered on top of standard TKI plus immunotherapy for kidney cancer.
explain.interesting: CAIX is about as clean a kidney-cancer target as exists, and combining radiation with IO is the current frontier for turning cold tumors hot.
explain.catch: Three active drugs at once muddies attribution, and antibody radioconjugates dose the marrow harder than small molecules do.

cards.title: Clarity's Copper-Based PSMA Theranostic Enters the Lutetium-Dominated Prostate Race
cards.hook: Nearly every PSMA therapy runs on gallium and lutetium, and Clarity is betting on a copper-64 and copper-67 pair held in a cage chelator instead. The pitch is a longer imaging window and a supply chain that does not lean on the same reactors everyone else is fighting over. Whether a different isotope turns into better outcomes is the part nobody has shown yet.
cards.shownBecause: biotech news — radiopharma pipeline moves

explain.snippet: Phase 1/2a theranostic study of 64Cu-SAR-bisPSMA and 67Cu-SAR-bisPSMA for imaging and treating PSMA-positive metastatic castrate-resistant prostate cancer.
explain.gist: A copper-pair PSMA agent using a sarcophagine cage chelator, positioned as an alternative to the gallium/lutetium standard.
explain.interesting: Copper-64 gives a longer imaging window and the isotope supply story is genuinely different, which matters in a field bottlenecked on reactor time.
explain.catch: Isotope novelty is not efficacy. The theranostic logic is sound but the outcome data against entrenched Lu-PSMA is not in yet.

cards.title: Statins Deplete Mitochondrial Coenzyme Q in Astrocytes and Shift Their Redox Balance
cards.hook: Statins block the mevalonate pathway to lower cholesterol, but that same pathway also builds coenzyme Q, and this paper watches astrocyte mitochondria lose CoQ and drift redox-wise as a result. It is the mechanistic version of the long-running argument about whether statin users should be topping up CoQ10. Doesn't mean anyone's brain is suffering on statins though, since this is cells in a dish and the leap to people is large.
cards.shownBecause: biochem — mitochondria topics you engage with

explain.snippet: Statin-induced mitochondrial coenzyme Q deficiency alters redox homeostasis and bioenergetic function in astrocytes.
explain.gist: The mevalonate pathway statins inhibit also makes CoQ. In astrocytes, that shared bottleneck shows up as measurable mitochondrial CoQ loss.
explain.interesting: It gives a concrete cellular handle on the statin-and-CoQ10 debate that has run on vibes for years, specifically in a brain cell type.
explain.catch: Astrocytes in culture, not people on statins. It says the mechanism is plausible, not that supplementation does anything clinically.

cards.title: AMP Deaminase-2 Structure Pins Down How Fructose Metabolism Is Regulated
cards.hook: Fructose catabolism burns through ATP and dumps AMP, and AMPD2 is the enzyme that decides what happens next, tuned by ATP, GTP and phosphate. This is the structure plus the activators, which is the groundwork anyone would want before trying to drug it for metabolic disease. Structure papers are a starting line and not a finish, so temper the druggable-target excitement.
cards.shownBecause: biochem — metabolism papers you save

explain.snippet: Structure, activators, and regulators of AMP deaminase-2 underpinning human fructose and nucleotide metabolism.
explain.gist: AMPD2 controls the fate of AMP produced when cells process fructose. This work resolves its structure and how ATP/GTP/Pi flip it on and off.
explain.interesting: Fructose handling is central to metabolic disease, and a regulated, structurally-defined enzyme at the choke point is exactly what medicinal chemists look for.
explain.catch: A structure and a mechanism, not a drug. The distance from here to a molecule that safely tunes AMPD2 in people is enormous.

cards.title: NanoBridge: Installing Post-Translational Modifications on Proteins That Usually Resist It
cards.hook: Editing a specific modification onto an endogenous protein inside a living cell is genuinely hard, and this group built a bridging system to force the modification onto proteins that normally will not cooperate. If it is as general as the abstract implies, it is a real tool for asking what a given modification actually does. Tool papers live or die on reproducibility though, and the abstract's phrase about challenging proteins is doing some quiet lifting.
cards.shownBecause: biochem — chemical biology, adjacent to your saves

explain.snippet: The NanoBridge system for targeted post-translational modification of otherwise intractable endogenous proteins.
explain.gist: A method for installing specific PTMs onto proteins in cells, aimed at targets that existing approaches cannot reliably modify.
explain.interesting: Being able to add one defined modification to one endogenous protein on demand is the clean experiment PTM biology usually can't run.
explain.catch: Generality is claimed, not proven across many targets, and cell-based editing tools often work beautifully in the founding lab and nowhere else.

cards.title: Engineered Biosensors to Push Monoterpene Indole Alkaloid Production in Yeast
cards.hook: Vinblastine and reserpine come from plant alkaloid pathways that are a nightmare to run in a microbe, and this group wired up biosensors so yeast strains making more of the intermediates light up and can be screened fast. It is the same brew-drugs-in-yeast dream as the paclitaxel work. The catch is that alkaloid titers are notoriously tiny, and a sensor that finds your best strain still doesn't make that strain good enough.
cards.shownBecause: org chem — biosynthesis papers you've saved

explain.snippet: Engineering transcription-factor biosensors to enhance monoterpene indole alkaloid production in Saccharomyces cerevisiae.
explain.gist: Biosensors that report intracellular alkaloid intermediates, letting you screen thousands of engineered yeast strains for the best producers.
explain.interesting: Complex plant alkaloids are brutal to make chemically. A fast readout for strain quality is what turns metabolic engineering from art into throughput.
explain.catch: Screening speed is not titer. MIA yields in yeast start microscopic, and a good sensor can still leave you far short of a viable process.

cards.title: Vinblastine (C46H58N4O9)
cards.hook: Vinblastine is the vinca alkaloid that stops cell division by jamming microtubule assembly, and it is a genuinely baroque molecule to have come out of a periwinkle plant. It pairs with the yeast-biosynthesis push, since this is exactly the kind of structure people are trying to brew instead of extract. Worth noting the yield from the plant is famously terrible, which is half the reason the synthetic-biology route matters at all.
cards.shownBecause: org chem — matches structures you've opened

explain.snippet: Vinblastine (C46H58N4O9), a dimeric vinca alkaloid from Catharanthus roseus. Microtubule-destabilizing chemotherapeutic, approved since the 1960s.
explain.gist: A bulky bisindole alkaloid that binds tubulin and blocks the mitotic spindle, halting dividing cells.
explain.interesting: That a plant makes something this structurally elaborate is exactly why chemists keep trying to reconstruct the pathway in engineered hosts.
explain.catch: Plant extraction gives vanishingly small yields, which is why it is expensive and why the microbial-production work has a real reason to exist.

cards.title: Genetically Encoded Protein Oscillators That Stream Data Like FM Radio
cards.hook: Someone built protein oscillators inside single cells and used frequency modulation, the same trick FM radio uses, to carry information in how fast they oscillate rather than how bright they get. It is the kind of thing that is either a foundational tool for reading cell state or a very elegant demo that stays a demo. Either way it is a genuinely odd idea done well, which is what the wildcard slot is for.
cards.shownBecause: wildcard — outside your usual lanes, on purpose

explain.snippet: Single-cell FM streaming using genetically encoded protein oscillators as biochemical carriers of information.
explain.gist: Protein clocks whose oscillation frequency, not amplitude, encodes a signal, borrowing frequency modulation from radio engineering.
explain.interesting: Amplitude readouts drift and bleach; frequency is robust. Porting that idea into cells is a clever answer to a real measurement problem.
explain.catch: Proof of concept in engineered cells. Whether anyone builds a useful sensor on top of it is the open question.

cards.title: A Reaction That Turns Condensates Into Vesicles and Sorts RNA While It Happens
cards.hook: This is protocell chemistry: a reaction drives liquid condensates to convert into membrane-bound vesicles, and along the way it selects and activates certain RNAs over others. It is the sort of result origin-of-life people get excited about, since compartments plus selection is most of the story they are trying to tell. It proves the chemistry can happen, not that it did, which is the eternal asterisk on this whole field.
cards.shownBecause: wildcard — origin-of-life chemistry, off your path

explain.snippet: A reaction-driven condensate-to-vesicle transition that selects, activates, and spatially organizes RNA (Devaraj lab).
explain.gist: A chemical reaction pushes phase-separated droplets to become membrane compartments, concentrating and activating specific RNAs in the process.
explain.interesting: Getting compartmentalization and molecular selection out of one spontaneous process is a tidy step in the protocell story.
explain.catch: Demonstrating a plausible prebiotic route is not evidence it was the actual one. That gap never fully closes in origin-of-life work.

cards.title: ProNotch: Turning Extracellular Protease Activity Into Programmable Gene Expression
cards.hook: This is a synthetic receptor that senses protease activity outside the cell and converts it into a transcriptional output you get to define, extending the synthetic-Notch toolkit. Proteases turn up all over disease, so a modular way to wire protease-is-active to turn-this-gene-on is a nice primitive to have. It is a platform paper, so the real test is whether other labs can make it fire cleanly without months of tuning.
cards.shownBecause: wildcard — cell engineering, off your path

explain.snippet: ProNotch converts extracellular protease activity into programmable transcriptional outputs in mammalian cells.
explain.gist: A synthetic receptor that detects a protease outside the cell and drives a gene circuit of your choosing in response.
explain.interesting: Protease activity marks a lot of disease states, so a clean sense-and-respond module opens up smart-cell therapy designs.
explain.catch: Synthetic receptors are finicky, and a platform's value is only proven once it works in hands other than the inventors'.

cards.title: Feeding Waste PET Plastic and Xylose to Oil-Making Microbes
cards.hook: Two oil-accumulating microbes are put to work turning broken-down PET plastic and xylose sugar into something useful, which is the industrial-biotech version of dealing with plastic waste instead of just burying it. The appeal is using the carbon locked in the waste rather than paying to landfill it. Whether it survives contact with real mixed and dirty waste streams, and with actual economics, is the part these papers rarely settle.
cards.shownBecause: biotech news — industrial biotech, wider net

explain.snippet: Integrated valorisation of PET and xylose using the oleaginous microbes Yarrowia lipolytica and Rhodococcus jostii.
explain.gist: Microbes that store lipids are fed depolymerized PET and a plant sugar, converting waste carbon into microbial oils.
explain.interesting: Upcycling plastic into a product beats degrading it to nothing, and pairing PET with xylose uses two waste streams at once.
explain.catch: Clean lab feedstocks are not municipal waste. Contamination, mixed plastics, and cost are where most of these processes stall.
